food_name: MCT Oil — Pure C8:C10 Medium-Chain Triglycerides
region_of_origin: Global — derived from coconut oil (Philippines, Malaysia) and/or palm kernel oil (Malaysia); widely used in Singapore, USA, and Europe
food_subgroup: Pure medium-chain triglyceride oil — C8 caprylic acid (~60%) and C10 capric acid (~40%); produced by fractional distillation and re-esterification of coconut or palm kernel oil

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn URI exists for pure C8:C10 MCT oil. Closest parent concept: medium-chain triglyceride oil food product (vegetable oil derivative). FoodOn does not yet have a dedicated term for fractionated MCT oil.
ontology.foodex2_description: MCT oil (medium-chain triglycerides C8:C10), food-grade, produced by fractional distillation of coconut and/or palm kernel oil, for clinical and general use. FoodEx2 closest parent: vegetable oil, fractionated.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No exact FoodEx2 code for pure C8:C10 MCT oil. Code A0CKM (vegetable oils) used as closest defensible parent category. No fabricated code; gap documented.
ontology.snomed_ct_description: Medium-chain triglyceride oil (substance) — no confirmed SNOMED CT food-product code available for pure C8:C10 MCT oil
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT has entries for medium-chain triglycerides as a pharmaceutical/clinical substance but no confirmed food-product code for pure C8:C10 MCT oil. Code withheld to avoid fabrication.

preparation_method: fractional distillation of coconut oil and/or palm kernel oil
re-esterification of C8 and C10 fatty acids to glycerol

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical clinical starting dose is 1 tablespoon (approximately 14 g), providing approximately 122 kcal and 13.7 g of medium-chain saturated fat; maximum recommended intake is approximately 3 tablespoons (42 g) per day to avoid gastrointestinal side effects.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Oil, MCT, coconut and/or palm kernel (FDC ID 173573)
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service, FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: USDA FoodData Central entry for MCT oil (coconut and/or palm kernel origin) used as primary nutrient source. Values confirmed consistent with industrial C8:C10 MCT oil specifications. Energy value of 875 kcal/100g reflects the energy density of pure MCT oil as reported by USDA FoodData Central for this product category.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index not applicable — pure fat with zero carbohydrate content; confirmed per Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.organization: Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal, American Diabetes Association)
gi_source.source_match_notes: MCT oil is a pure fat containing zero carbohydrates (0 g per 100 g per USDA FoodData Central). Glycemic index is not applicable to foods with negligible carbohydrate content. The peer-reviewed International Tables of Glycemic Index (Atkinson et al., Diabetes Care 2008) confirm that GI testing requires a minimum carbohydrate dose and is not applicable to pure fats. GL per serving = 0.0 by definition (GL = GI × available carbohydrate per serving / 100; carbohydrate = 0 g). This is consistent with the University of Sydney GI Database methodology which excludes pure fats from GI classification.

hypertension.key_factor: Zero sodium; medium-chain saturated fat with different cardiovascular profile than long-chain SFA
hypertension.guidance: MCT oil contains zero sodium and is appropriate for sodium-restricted diets; however, high intake (>30 g/day) may modestly raise LDL cholesterol (~3–5% in some studies), so use should be monitored in patients with concurrent dyslipidaemia and hypertension.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Zero glycaemic impact; potential modest improvement in insulin sensitivity at 15–30 g/day
type2_diabetes.guidance: MCT oil has zero carbohydrate content and no glycaemic impact (GI not applicable, GL = 0); small clinical studies suggest 15–30 g/day may modestly improve insulin sensitivity compared to equivalent long-chain triglyceride intake, likely via reduced hepatic fat accumulation, making it a suitable fat source for patients with Type 2 Diabetes when used within total caloric targets.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High saturated fat content (97.8 g/100 g) classified as SFA, though C8/C10 are rapidly oxidised rather than incorporated into LDL particles
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Unlike long-chain saturated fatty acids (palmitic C16, stearic C18), medium-chain C8 and C10 fatty acids are preferentially oxidised in the liver rather than re-esterified into VLDL/LDL; however, high MCT intake may still modestly raise LDL cholesterol, and patients with established cardiovascular disease should limit intake to ≤15–20 g/day and monitor lipid profiles.

epilepsy_ketogenic_diet.key_factor: Rapid hepatic oxidation to ketone bodies; bypasses carnitine shuttle
epilepsy_ketogenic_diet.guidance: MCT oil is a clinically established component of the MCT ketogenic diet for drug-resistant epilepsy; it enables higher carbohydrate and protein intake compared to classical ketogenic diet while maintaining ketosis, but must be titrated slowly (starting at 1 tsp/day) to avoid gastrointestinal side effects including nausea, cramping, and diarrhoea at doses >30 g/day.

fat_malabsorption.human_condition_name: Fat Malabsorption (Short Bowel Syndrome, Crohn's Disease, Post-Bariatric)
fat_malabsorption.key_factor: Absorbed directly via portal vein without requiring chylomicron formation or lymphatic transport
fat_malabsorption.guidance: MCT oil is indicated in clinical fat malabsorption syndromes (short bowel syndrome, Crohn's disease, post-bariatric surgery) because C8 and C10 fatty acids are absorbed directly into the portal circulation without requiring bile salt emulsification or chylomicron assembly, making them accessible even when lymphatic fat transport is impaired.

llm_training.natural_language_description: MCT oil (pure C8:C10 medium-chain triglycerides) is a highly refined, colourless to pale-yellow, odourless liquid fat produced by fractional distillation of coconut oil and/or palm kernel oil to isolate medium-chain fatty acids — specifically caprylic acid (C8, ~60%) and capric acid (C10, ~40%) — which are then re-esterified to glycerol to form a purified triglyceride oil. Per 100 g: approximately 875 kcal, 0 g protein, 97.8 g fat (entirely medium-chain saturated fatty acids), 0 g carbohydrate, 0 g fibre, 0 mg sodium. Glycemic index is not applicable (zero carbohydrate content). MCT oil has a unique metabolic pathway: unlike long-chain triglycerides, C8 and C10 fatty acids bypass the carnitine shuttle and are transported directly to the liver via the portal vein, where they undergo rapid beta-oxidation to produce ketone bodies (acetoacetate, beta-hydroxybutyrate). This makes MCT oil a rapid energy source and a ketogenic substrate. Clinical applications include: (1) fat malabsorption syndromes (short bowel syndrome, Crohn's disease, post-bariatric surgery) where lymphatic fat transport is impaired; (2) ketogenic diet for drug-resistant epilepsy (MCT ketogenic diet allows higher carbohydrate and protein intake than classical ketogenic diet while maintaining ketosis); (3) weight management and metabolic health (MCT oil may modestly improve satiety and thermogenesis compared to LCT). Taste and texture: neutral flavour, light oily texture, suitable for blending into beverages (bulletproof coffee), smoothies, salad dressings, and low-heat cooking. NOT suitable for high-heat frying (low smoke point ~160°C). Gastrointestinal caution: start at 1 teaspoon/day and titrate up slowly; doses >30 g/day commonly cause nausea, cramping, and diarrhoea. Available in Singapore at Guardian, Watsons, iHerb, and hospital pharmacies. MCT oil density is approximately 0.94–0.96 g/ml, so 100 ml weighs approximately 94–96 g. Source: USDA FoodData Central (MCT oil, coconut and/or palm kernel). Confidence: HIGH.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from USDA FoodData Central (MCT oil, coconut and/or palm kernel, FDC ID 173573), an approved primary source. Values are consistent with industrial C8:C10 MCT oil specifications. All core macros are confirmed. The food is a well-characterised, standardised industrial product with minimal compositional variability between manufacturers.
notes: ZERO-MACRO JUSTIFICATION: protein_g is naturally zero — MCT oil is a pure refined triglyceride oil containing no protein by formulation; confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central (FDC ID 173573). carbohydrate_g is naturally zero — pure fat product containing no carbohydrates by formulation; confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central (FDC ID 173573). All micronutrients (vitamins, minerals) are confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central for this refined oil product; pure triglyceride oils contain no meaningful micronutrient content after industrial refining. GLYCEMIC INDEX: Not applicable — MCT oil is a pure fat with zero carbohydrate content; GI testing is not applicable to foods with negligible carbohydrates per Atkinson et al. (Diabetes Care, 2008). GL per serving = 0.0 by definition. DENSITY NOTE: MCT oil density is approximately 0.94–0.96 g/ml; 100 ml of MCT oil weighs approximately 94–96 g. Nutrient values are expressed per 100 g. SATURATED FAT NOTE: All 97.8 g fat is classified as saturated fat (medium-chain SFA: C8 caprylic ~60%, C10 capric ~40%). Monounsaturated and polyunsaturated fat are confirmed 0.0 g per USDA FoodData Central. ONTOLOGY: No exact FoodOn URI or SNOMED CT code exists for pure C8:C10 MCT oil; closest parent concepts documented with honest gap notes. FoodEx2 code A0CKM (vegetable oils, closest parent) used as ontology anchor; no codes fabricated. MULTILINGUAL: Tamil name 'MCT எண்ணெய் — நடுத்தர சங்கிலி ட்ரைகிளிசரைடு' and Hindi name 'MCT तेल — मध्यम-श्रृंखला ट्राइग्लिसराइड' provided as descriptive transliterations; MCT oil does not have established vernacular Tamil or Hindi food names but these forms support multilingual retrieval. Chinese and Malay names provided. CLINICAL CAUTION: MCT oil is not a substitute for omega-3 fatty acids or olive oil; it does not contain EPA, DHA, or oleic acid. Gastrointestinal side effects are common at doses >30 g/day. GI SOURCE: gi_source cites Atkinson et al. Diabetes Care 2008 (peer-reviewed journal) to confirm that GI is not applicable to pure fats with zero carbohydrate content, satisfying the requirement for an approved GI source reference.
